# FRAMEWORK PDF CAPTIONS — cub-hurricanes 2025-08-26
# framework_doc: https://www.unocha.org/attachments/daccda29-d7d9-4f24-82f7-088e561b43d6/CUBA_Marco%20AA%20huracanes_Aprobado%20ERC.pdf
# pages: 40 · model: sonnet · via: claude headless
# Auto-generated descriptions of the PDF's visuals (maps/charts/tables). PUBLIC.
------------------------------------------------------------
--- page 1 ---
Title page and table of contents. Document: "Marco de acción anticipatoria Cuba – Huracanes, Versión 1 – 26/08/2025." Approved by CR/HC 26/08/2025 and ERC 27/08/2025. Sections listed: Executive Summary; 1. Tormentas y huracanes en Cuba (p3); 2. Acuerdos de coordinación (p5); 3. El mecanismo de activación (p7) — including trigger development, forecast sources, thresholds, activation protocol; 4. Enfoque geográfico (p13); 5. La entrega: plan de acción anticipatoria (p14) — criteria, objectives, assistance summary, readiness for implementation; 6. Financiación preestablecida (p19) — CERF, donor complementarity, considerations; 7. El aprendizaje (p23); 8. Disposiciones finales (p24).

--- page 2 ---
Table of contents continued. Sections: 7. El aprendizaje — Seguimiento y evaluación (p23), Lecciones de otros marcos (p23); 8. Disposiciones finales — Vigencia y futuro del marco (p24), Incidencia y comunicación pública (p24). Annexes: Annex 1 – Desarrollo de disparadores y desempeño histórico (p25); Annex 2 – Definición de ZMA (p28); Annex 3 – Borrador de mensajes disparadores (p29). Narrative text, no figure.

--- page 3 ---
Executive Summary. Framework pre-arranges US$4 million CERF pooled-fund financing (2025–2026) to protect up to 170,000 people in Cuba. Three trigger types defined. Table 1 — Triggers Overview:
| Trigger type              | Lead time             | Wind speed         | Rainfall |
| Forecast: Readiness       | Up to 120 hours (5d) | 120 knots (Cat. 4) | n/a      |
| Forecast: Anticipatory Action | Up to 72 hours (3d) | 120 knots (Cat. 4) | n/a  |
| Observation: Early Response | n/a                 | 105 knots (Cat. 3) | 98.2 mm  |
Forecast triggers use official NOAA NHC forecast track entering INSMET's ZMA. Observational trigger requires observed NHC track through ZMA at ≥105 knots AND 2-day total precipitation ≥98.2 mm in ≥20% of the country.

--- page 4 ---
Executive Summary continued. Table 2 — Trigger Mechanism Statistics (analysis years 2000–2024):
| Trigger       | Return period | Activation probability | Years activated |
| Readiness     | 3.7 years     | 27%                   | 2001 (Michelle), 2004 (Ivan), 2005 (Dennis, Wilma), 2008 (Gustav, Ike, Paloma), 2016 (Matthew), 2017 (Irma), 2022 (Ian) |
| Action        | 3.7 years     | 27%                   | Same as above |
| Observational | 3.7 years     | 27%                   | Same as above minus Wilma (2005) and Gustav (2008) |
Action plan covers 6 sectors: food security, logistics, WASH, education, health, shelter. Four objectives: temporary population protection (evacuation support), in-kind assistance, supply mobilization/storage, livelihood protection and basic services access. Validity: hurricane seasons 2025 and 2026 (until 31 December 2026).

--- page 5 ---
Section 1: Tormentas y huracanes en Cuba. Main point: Cuba's exposure to tropical cyclones shown via historical storm track spaghetti map.
MAP — Figure 1: All tropical storms and hurricanes that passed within 250 km of Cuba (1981–2022). Area shown: Caribbean basin, Gulf of Mexico, western Atlantic, Cuba at center. Color-coded track lines by Saffir-Simpson category: Cat 5 (darkest red), Cat 4, Cat 3, Cat 2, Cat 1, Tropical Storm, Tropical Depression, Extratropical. Dense overlapping tracks; western Cuba notably more affected. Named storms visible include: Irma 2017, Sandy 2002, Floyd 1981, Andrew 1992, Ivan 2004, Michelle 2001, Matthew 2016, Charley 2004, and many others. Source: implied NOAA/NHC historical track data.

--- page 6 ---
Section 1 continued. Main point: Cat 4 hurricanes account for the largest share (30%) of systems hitting Cuba 1925–2024; average affected population rises sharply with category.
CHART — Figure 2: Bar chart "Percentage of tropical systems that hit Cuba between 1925 and 2024 by category" (source: EMDAT).
X-axis: Tropical Storm, Cat 1, Cat 2, Cat 3, Cat 4, Cat 5.
Y-axis: percentage (0–35%).
Values: Tropical Storm 21.74%, Cat 1 8.70%, Cat 2 17.39%, Cat 3 8.70%, Cat 4 30.43%, Cat 5 13.04%.
Humanitarian impact tiers (EMDAT-based):
- Moderate (Cat ≤2): avg 111,962 affected; max 476,891 (Kate 1985).
- Severe (Cat 3): avg 1,681,303 affected; min 162,605; max 3,200,000 (Ian 2022).
- Extreme (Cat ≥4): avg 2,577,934 affected; min 49,445; max 10,000,000 (Irma 2017).

--- page 7 ---
Section 2: Coordination arrangements. Narrative text, no figure. Describes UN–government coordination structure; OCHA leads process under RC; WFP leads logistics and co-leads food security with FAO; UNICEF leads WASH and education; PAHO/OPS leads health; UNDP supports housing. Cuba UN system has active OCR and UNETE. Agencies coordinating with national counterparts (MINCEX, EMNDC, INSMET, INRH, Defensa Civil).

--- page 8 ---
Section 2 continued (Alignment with national priorities + Synergies). Section 3 begins: El mecanismo de activación — Trigger development process and Forecast/observation data sources. Narrative text, no figure. Data sources: forecast track from NOAA NHC official forecast; observed track from NOAA NHC official best track; observed precipitation from NASA IMERG v7b daily precipitation. Code publicly available at https://github.com/OCHA-DAP/ds-aa-cub-hurricanes.

--- page 9 ---
Section 3: Activation and thresholds (Spanish main text). Main point: three-trigger structure with thresholds defined.
Table 3 — General trigger description (Spanish):
| Tipo de disparador           | Plazo de entrega   | Velocidad del viento | Precipitación   |
| Pronóstico: Alistamiento     | Hasta 120 horas (5d) | 120 nudos (Cat. 4) | N/A             |
| Pronóstico: Acción anticipatoria | Hasta 72 horas (3d) | 120 nudos (Cat. 4) | N/A          |
| Observación: Respuesta temprana | N/A              | 105 nudos (Cat. 3) | 98.2 milímetros |
Multiple activations in one year count as a single event for return period calculation.

--- page 10 ---
Section 3: Activation mechanism statistics and start of activation protocol. Main point: trigger accuracy metrics and step-by-step protocol by actor.
Table 4 — Activation mechanism statistics: identical structure to Table 2 (3.7-year return period, 27% probability for all three triggers, same activation years).
Table 5 — Accuracy metrics (analysis 2000–2024, 9 target storms = top-impact storms since 2000, 23 total storms):
| Trigger              | Accuracy | Detection rate | False alarm rate | Precision | F1 score |
| Readiness + Action   | 70%      | 67%            | 29%             | 60%       | 63%      |
| Observational        | 74%      | 56%            | 14%             | 71%       | 63%      |
Activation protocol table begins (columns: QUIÉN, QUÉ, CÓMO, CUÁNDO); hurricane season monitoring row: OCHA-CHD shares forecast evolution, monitors triggers, maintains dialogue with INSMET and UN agencies via regular follow-up, WhatsApp, email throughout hurricane season.

--- page 11 ---
Section 3: Activation protocol table — Readiness window (−5 days). Main point: step-by-step actors and actions when readiness trigger is reached.
Table (4 columns: Actor | Action | Method | Timing) for "SI SE ALCANZA EL DISPARADOR DE ALISTAMIENTO (−5 DÍAS)":
- OCHA-CHD: technical validation with INSMET; sends provisional activation email to INSMET, OCHA, OCR. Via email + WhatsApp. When forecast approaches readiness threshold.
- INSMET+EMNDC+MINCEX+CR+OCHA: validates activation jointly; RC decides in dialogue with national authorities (MINCEX+EMNDC). Via email, WhatsApp, phone. Once threshold reached, within 3–5 hours.
- Coordinador Residente (CR)-OCR: officially confirms activation to CERF; informs agencies. Email to CERF (incl. OCHA units) + agencies. As soon as CHD notification sent and national validation confirmed.
- OCHA ROLAC: deploys field team to support OCR Cuba in key roles. Via RC email activating emergency personnel deployment processes. As soon as RC confirms activation.
- CERF Secretariat: sends approval letters authorizing only the readiness-percentage use of funds immediately. Letters to each agency cc RC, OCHA ROLAC. As soon as readiness threshold reached and immediately after RC confirmation.
- CR + UN Agencies + EMNDC/MINCEX + sector counterparts: begin implementing readiness window actions. Joint, coordinated manner via OCR/UNETE meetings. As soon as RC confirms activation.

--- page 12 ---
Section 3: Activation protocol table — Anticipatory Action window (−3 days) and Observational trigger (+24 hours). Main point: distinct protocols for each trigger type with CERF fund authorization rules.
Anticipatory Action window (−3 DÍAS):
- OCHA-CHD, INSMET+EMNDC+MINCEX+CR+OCHA, CR-OCR, OCHA ROLAC: same roles as readiness protocol.
- CERF Secretariat: if action trigger reached WITHOUT readiness: sends approval letters authorizing 100% of funds; if action reached AFTER readiness: sends email confirming agencies authorized to use full CERF allocation.
- CR + Agencies + sector counterparts: implement anticipatory action window activities.
Observational trigger (+24 hours, ONLY activated if anticipatory action window was NOT previously activated):
- OCHA-CHD: validates with INSMET and INRH; sends provisional notification. As soon as phenomenon makes landfall and first wind/precipitation indicators measurable.
- INSMET+INRH+EMNDC+MINCEX+OCR: validates; RC decides. Via email, WhatsApp, phone. Once early response trigger reached.
- CR-OCR, OCHA ROLAC: same as above.
- CERF Secretariat: if early response reached WITHOUT any prior windows: authorizes 100% of funds; if after readiness window: confirms full allocation authorized.

--- page 13 ---
Section 3 conclusion + Section 4: Geographic focus. Main point: geographic targeting is flexible and government-confirmed; late intensification scenario (action trigger without prior readiness) explicitly addressed.
No figure. Late intensification note: agencies proceed directly with combined protocol; no cut-off delivery time agreed. Geographic focus: flexible based on forecast track; priority to municipalities/communities with highest vulnerability and exposure per PVR (Peligro, Vulnerabilidad y Riesgo) studies and EMNDC database. Six sectors prioritized: food security (incl. agriculture), logistics, health, WASH, education, housing. Government defines/confirms priority municipalities. Beneficiary selection criteria listed: highest vulnerability municipalities; flood-affected populations historically; energy-supply disruption vulnerability; activated evacuation centers; sick persons in evacuation centers; high-vulnerability health centers; limited-capacity medical brigades; vulnerable water-supply populations; flood-risk schools; vulnerable housing; persons with disability.

--- page 14 ---
Section 4 beneficiary criteria continued + Section 5: La entrega — Plan de acción anticipatoria. Main point: action selection criteria and four delivery objectives defined.
No figure. Selection criteria: anticipatory character; feasible within opportunity window; operational capacity; no-regrets approach; alignment with government anticipatory actions. Four objectives:
1. Support activities related to temporary population protection (evacuation): food + NFI (hygiene kits, lighting, collapsible tanks, early childhood kits, mattresses) to protection centers and relocated people per Directive 1.
2. Support in-kind assistance: cooking kits + food (rice, grains, oil) to food preparation centers; hygiene kits + water storage tanks to affected population.
3. Support mobilization and storage of basic equipment and supplies: lighting towers, water treatment plants; mobile storage units, pallets, tents.
4. Protect livelihoods and facilitate continued basic services access: protect homes and productive assets; food production continuity; health, education, energy access.

--- page 15 ---
Section 5 continued (one-sentence continuation of objective 4). Narrative text, no figure. Mentions food production continuity and basic services access (health, education, energy). Page is almost entirely blank — overflow text only.

--- page 16 ---
Section 5: Assistance summary — Cuadro sinóptico (Table 6). Main point: six-sector activity matrix mapping objectives, sectors, results, activities, and implementing agencies.
TABLE — Activities by sector and organization:
| Objective | Sector | Result | Activity | Organization |
| Temporary population protection | Food security | Support adequate food consumption | Food + NFI to activated government temporary protection centers | WFP |
| Temporary population protection | WASH | Adequate water/sanitation/hygiene services; prevent disease | Hygiene kits + water storage tanks to temporary protection centers | UNICEF |
| Temporary population protection | Shelter | Adequate shelter conditions | Basic shelter kits (tarps, roofing, tools), habitability items (mattresses, cooking kits) to centers and refuge housing | UNDP |
| In-kind assistance | Food security | Support adequate food consumption | Food + NFI (cooking kits) to food preparation centers | WFP |
| In-kind assistance | WASH | Adequate WASH services | Hygiene kits + water storage tanks to affected people | UNICEF |
| Equipment/supply mobilization | Logistics | Adequate transport + storage | Tents, mobile storage units, lighting towers, pallets, fuel | WFP |
| Equipment/supply mobilization | WASH | Adequate WASH + disease prevention | Transport + installation of portable water treatment plants | UNICEF |
| Livelihood protection | Food security-agriculture | Guarantee food production + protect livelihoods | Short-cycle seeds, agricultural inputs + tools, artisanal fishing kits (FAO); animal feed + veterinary services (FAO); cleanup + agricultural infrastructure repair (FAO) | FAO |

--- page 17 ---
Section 5: Assistance table continued (Table 6 rows for health, shelter, education). Main point: health, shelter, education sectors covered under Objective 4 (livelihood protection / basic services).
TABLE continuation:
| Objective | Sector | Result | Activity | Organization |
| Livelihood protection | Health | Ensure continued health services access | Distribution of critical medical supplies to health centers | OPS/PAHO |
| Livelihood protection | Shelter | Adequate conditions in temporary facilities | Generators + rechargeable lamps to population in temporary protection centers and refuge housing (to address electricity shortage) | UNDP |
| Livelihood protection | Education | Ensure continued educational services access | Early childhood kits + recreation kits; tarpaulins for temporary classrooms; roofing for rapid repair of affected education spaces | UNICEF |

--- page 18 ---
Section 5: Garantizar la preparación para una implementación oportuna (Readiness for timely implementation). Narrative text, no figure. Key points: significant pre-positioning effort given short action lead time; agencies increasing pre-positioned supplies and diversifying items in country; master agreements with suppliers; simulation exercises conducted; lessons from hurricanes Oscar and Rafael 2024–2025 incorporated; logistics subgroup established within UNETE; sanctions + economic crisis add supply chain challenges; framework helps accelerate restocking of distributed items.

--- page 19 ---
Section 6: Financiación preestablecida — CERF. Main point: $4 million CERF pre-arranged with three disbursement conditions and fund-use rules by trigger scenario.
No figure. ERC pre-arranged up to US$4 million CERF for 2025–2026 hurricane seasons. Three disbursement conditions: (1) framework + agency project/budget packages approved by RC; (2) framework + packages approved by ERC; (3) agreed triggers activated within 2 years of ERC approval. Fund use rules:
- Readiness trigger only: only readiness-assigned funds usable.
- Anticipatory action trigger: all funds usable for anticipatory actions + early response.
- Observational trigger without prior action trigger: all funds for early response.
- Readiness trigger but no subsequent action/observation: remaining funds stay with agencies until end of season; unconsumed funds returned to CERF if no further trigger reached.
CERF pre-arrangement does not preclude CERF rapid response access for other unforeseen crises in Cuba.

--- page 20 ---
Section 6: Financing model diagram + probable scenarios table. Main point: five scenarios map which triggers activate and what percentage of funds is released.
DIAGRAM — Figure 3: Financing model flowchart. Shows three trigger branches (Readiness, Anticipatory Action/Crisis, Early Response) → CERF disbursement node → outputs (Readiness activities; Action: distributions + services). Annotation: "Action trigger: ONE or OTHER (anticipatory action or early response) — only ONE of the two can be reached."
TABLE — 5 probable scenarios:
| Scenario | Situation | Triggers | Fund use |
| 1 Readiness | Forecast changes/weakens, no Cuba landfall at threshold | Only readiness reached (no action, no early response) | 10% no-regrets |
| 2 Readiness + Anticipatory Action | Forecasts predict Cuba landfall; track changes or weakens below threshold | Readiness + action (no observational) | 100% |
| 3 Readiness + Early Response | Forecast indicates potential impact; changes direction/intensity, lands in Cuba with high wind + heavy rain | Readiness + early response (observational; action threshold not met) | 100% |
| 4 Anticipatory Action only (late intensification) | Late intensification forecast; action trigger reached without readiness | Action only (no readiness, no observational) | 100% |
| 5 Early Response only | Hurricane not forecast to reach thresholds; lands with high wind + heavy rain meeting observational thresholds | Only observational reached (no readiness, no action) | 100% |

--- page 21 ---
Section 6: Pre-established financing tables by sector and by agency. Main point: $4 million total distributed across 5 agencies and 6 sectors.
TABLE 6 — Pre-established financing by sector (USD):
| Sector | CERF fund (USD) | Co-financing (USD) | Total (USD) |
| Food security | 1,350,000 (WFP $840,000 + FAO $510,000) | 217,540 (WFP) | 1,350,000 |
| WASH | 300,000 | — | 300,000 |
| Shelter/Housing | 1,000,000 | — | 1,000,000 |
| Health | 440,000 | — | 440,000 |
| Education | 250,000 | — | 250,000 |
| Logistics | 660,000 | — | 660,000 |
TABLE 7 — CERF financing by agency and sector:
| Agency | Sector | Financing | People targeted | Agency total |
| OPS/PAHO | Health | $440,000 (11%) | 24,000 | $440,000 |
| FAO | Food security-agriculture | $510,000 (13%) | 15,268 | $510,000 |
| UNICEF | WASH | $300,000 (7.5%) | 25,000 | $550,000 |
| UNICEF | Education | $250,000 (6.25%) | 8,200 | |
| WFP | Food security | $840,000 (21%) | 170,000 | $1,500,000 |
| WFP | Logistics | $660,000 (16.5%) | 170,000 | |
| UNDP | Housing | $1,000,000 (25%) | 75,000 | $1,000,000 |
| TOTAL | | $4,000,000 (100%) | | $4,000,000 |
(People figures are approximate planning figures.)

--- page 22 ---
Section 6: Considerations. Narrative text, no figure. Geographic scope and impact difficult to predict at low uncertainty levels; RC may consider requesting CERF rapid response window if formal thresholds not reached but significant humanitarian needs arise. Framework build/enablement costs (collective targeting, logistics strategies) not traditionally funded by pooled funds including CERF; borne by participating agencies. Pre-positioning strategy requires significant effort given sanctions and economic crisis logistics barriers; framework adds value by accelerating restocking of distributed supplies.

--- page 23 ---
Section 7: El aprendizaje (Learning). Narrative text, no figure. M&E: each agency uses own M&E systems; OCHA 3W system for joint monitoring; supply tracking matrix from prior responses; joint after-action review; agencies collectively agreed to budget a joint learning exercise line proportional to their budgets. Lessons applied from other frameworks and activations: inclusion of observational component with rainfall trigger (given unpredictable cyclone nature); broad geographic flexibility; flexibility in activity implementation beyond the opportunity window.

--- page 24 ---
Section 8: Disposiciones finales (Final provisions). Narrative text, no figure. Validity: framework valid for 2 hurricane seasons (2025 and 2026); 2025 is pilot year for inter-institutional pre-positioning management; 2026 season lessons applied to improve the framework (trigger mechanism, financing, co-financing, action plan, geographic scope). CERF financing pre-arranged for activations during this period. Advocacy and communication: agencies coordinate key messages under government and RC leadership; agencies share photos, videos, impact stories with OCR and OCHA as available.

--- page 25 ---
Annex 1: Trigger development and historical performance — optimization process. Narrative text, no figure. Starting point: ZMA definition. Optimization iterated over 3,897,600 possible trigger combinations for action and observational triggers independently; filtered to 337,851 meeting return-period constraint (7 activation years = 3.7-year period per CERF guidance); redundant options removed → 2,543; additional simplicity filters → 59 options. Variables to maximize: (1) sum of total affected people across historically triggered hurricanes; (2) total CERF allocations across triggered hurricanes. Constraint: exactly 7 activation years. Parameters iterated: max wind speed in ZMA (5-knot steps, NOAA NHC historical min to max); rainfall spatial aggregation (mean, median, P80, P90, P95, P99); rainfall value (all historical values). Data: NOAA NHC for wind (action + observational); CHIRPS-GEFS for forecast precipitation (action trigger); IMERG v7b 1-day for observed precipitation (observational). Rainfall period: 2-day total chosen (1-day and total storm options explored; 2-day captures rainfall peaks around midnight).

--- page 26 ---
Annex 1: Historical performance charts and interpretation. Main point: scatter plots show which historical storms would have activated each trigger type; Irma 2017 is the largest event; observational trigger historically redundant with action trigger.
CHARTS — Two side-by-side scatter plots:
LEFT — "Action (forecast)": X-axis: Max wind speed while in ZMA (knots), range 0–140+, with Saffir-Simpson thresholds marked (Tropical Storm 34kt, Cat1 64kt, Cat2 83kt, Cat3 96kt, Cat4 113kt, Cat5 137kt). Y-axis: Two-day rainfall, 80th quantile over whole country (mm), range 0–140mm. Threshold line at ~33.7mm rainfall (effectively no rainfall threshold — all storms exceed this). Threshold wind: 120 knots. Activated storms (bold): Irma 2017 (largest bubble, ~120kt, ~90mm), Michelle 2001, Ivan 2004, Dennis 2005, Wilma 2005, Paloma 2008, Ike 2008, Matthew 2016, Ian 2022. Red bubbles (had CERF): Irma 2017, Ian 2022, Ike 2008, Matthew 2016, Dennis 2005, Gustav 2008. Italic (activated by other trigger only): several storms.
RIGHT — "Observational": Same X-axis. Y-axis: Two-day rainfall, 80th quantile over whole country (mm), range 0–250+mm. Threshold line at ~100mm. Threshold wind: 105 knots. Activated storms: Michelle 2001, Ike 2008, Dennis 2005, Irma 2017, Paloma 2008, Matthew 2016, Ian 2022. Wilma 2005 does NOT appear in observational chart (excluded — no observational trigger). Alberto 2018 and June 2022 appear with high rainfall but low wind.
Bubble size proportional to "Total affected" people.

--- page 27 ---
Annex 1: Historical performance table. Main point: storm-level record of which triggers would have fired and CERF assignment status (23 storms, 2000–2024, sorted by total affected descending).
TABLE — Columns: Storm | Action trigger | Observational trigger | CERF | Total Affected:
Irma 2017: Trig. / Trig. / Yes / 10,000,000
Michelle 2001: Trig. / Trig. / pre- / 5,900,012
Rafael 2024: No trig. / No trig. / Yes / 4,000,000
Ian 2022: Trig. / Trig. / Yes / 3,200,000
Ike 2008: Trig. / Trig. / Yes / 2,600,000
Dennis 2005: Trig. / Trig. / pre- / 2,500,000
Gustav 2008: Trig. / No trig. / Yes / 450,019
Oscar 2024: No trig. / No trig. / Yes / 320,000
Lili 2002: No trig. / No trig. / pre- / 281,470
Charley 2004: No trig. / No trig. / pre- / 244,005
Noel 2007: No trig. / No trig. / No / 192,488
Matthew 2016: Trig. / Trig. / Yes / 190,000
Sandy 2012: No trig. / No trig. / Yes / 162,605
Wilma 2005: Trig. / No trig. / pre- / 100,000
Paloma 2008: Trig. / Trig. / No / 49,445
Isaac 2012: No trig. / No trig. / No / 45,496
Isidore 2002: No trig. / No trig. / pre- / 42,500
Alberto 2018: No trig. / No trig. / No / 40,000
Alex 2022: No trig. / No trig. / No / 6,780
Ivan 2004: Trig. / No trig. / pre- / 3,245
Michael 2018: No trig. / No trig. / No / 540
Isaias 2020: No trig. / No trig. / No / 500
Alberto 2006: No trig. / No trig. / No / 268
(pre- = storm pre-dates CERF establishment in 2006)

--- page 28 ---
Annex 2: ZMA definition. Main point: the Zona de Máxima Atención (ZMA) is INSMET/EMNDC's ~230 km band around Cuba used as the geographic trigger zone.
MAP — Shows Caribbean basin: Cuba at center; USA to north; Gulf of Mexico and Mexico to west/northwest; Yucatan Peninsula; Central America (Guatemala, Honduras, Nicaragua, Costa Rica, Panama); Colombia and Venezuela to south; Caribbean Sea; Jamaica and Hispaniola to east. ZMA boundary shown as red dotted line forming a polygon around Cuba and adjacent waters (~230 km band). Green shaded area = Zona de Vigilancia Reforzada (ZVR) — broader surveillance zone extending further from Cuba (bounded roughly by 60°W meridian to east, 24°N to north, Central American coast to west, Venezuela coast to south).
COORDINATE TABLE (upper right) — ZMA boundary vertex coordinates:
18.5°N 72.3°W; 18.5°N 78.0°W; 20.0°N 80.0°W; 20.0°N 85.0°W; 21.2°N 86.8°W; 23.0°N 86.0°W; 24.0°N 85.0°W; 24.0°N 81.6°W; 23.5°N 77.0°W; 21.0°N 72.3°W.

--- page 29 ---
Annex 3: Draft trigger messages — Readiness trigger CHD notification (Spanish + English). Main point: automated email template sent by OCHA CHD when readiness threshold reached.
Spanish version: From data.science@humdata.org; To: INSMET, OCHA, Coordinador Residente; Subject: "Acción Anticipatoria Cuba – Disparador de ALISTAMIENTO alcanzado / Anticipatory Action Cuba-READINESS trigger reached." Body: notifies that readiness window trigger has been reached per 120-hour NHC forecast; promises additional updates. Signed: Centro de Datos Humanitarios OCHA.
English version (same page): Identical structure; body states readiness trigger reached per 120-hour NHC forecast issued at 7:00 p.m., Dec. 31, 2024 (Cuba local time) — uses TEST_STORM_NAME placeholder.

--- page 30 ---
Annex 3: Readiness trigger CHD notification — English version (continued). Narrative text, no figure. Full English email body from OCHA Center for Humanitarian Data: confirms readiness trigger reached per 120-hour forecast; additional updates promised. Signed: OCHA Center for Humanitarian Data, Fluwelen Burgwal 58, 2511 CJ The Hague, Netherlands. Contact: Raquel Peña, raquel.pena@un.org.

--- page 31 ---
Annex 3: RC confirmation of Readiness window activation — Spanish + English. Main point: template for Resident Coordinator's official confirmation letter to CERF secretariat and agencies when readiness window activated.
Spanish: From RC; To: CERF Secretariat + agencies; Subject confirms READINESS window activation. Body: based on INSMET/CHD analysis, readiness trigger reached; RC confirms activation; requests CERF proceed with activation steps; invites agencies to immediately implement readiness actions. Key reminders: temporal focus (quick-impact resources, limited windows), accountability (CERF transparency standards), coordination (align with national/local authorities + other UN agencies).
English: Same content — confirms READINESS window activation; requests CERF next steps; invites agencies to implement immediately; agencies will receive CERF approval letters shortly. Signed: United Nations Resident Coordinator, Cuba.

--- page 32 ---
Annex 3: RC confirmation of Readiness window — English continuation. Narrative text, no figure. Key reminders about CERF Anticipatory Action funds (English): 1. Temporal focus — quick-impact resources, limited implementation windows; actions outside window time frames not accepted. 2. Accountability — report fund use per CERF transparency standards; share beneficiary lists as needed. 3. Coordination — critical to align actions with national and local authorities; also align with other UN agencies, NGOs, civil society. Signed: United Nations Resident Coordinator, Cuba.

--- page 33 ---
Annex 3: Anticipatory Action trigger CHD notification — Spanish + English. Main point: automated email template sent by OCHA CHD when anticipatory action (72-hour) threshold reached.
Spanish: From data.science@humdata.org; To: INSMET, OCHA, OCR (OCHA ROLAC added); Subject: "Disparador de ACCIÓN ANTICIPATORIA alcanzado / ANTICIPATORY ACTION trigger reached." Body: notifies anticipatory action trigger reached per 72-hour NHC forecast at 19h00 31 Dec 2024 Cuba local time.
English (begins on same page): "Cuba: Hurricane monitoring" — same content referencing 72-hour forecast; TEST_STORM_NAME placeholder used.

--- page 34 ---
Annex 3: Anticipatory Action trigger CHD notification — English version. Narrative text, no figure. Full English email from OCHA Center for Humanitarian Data: informs that readiness trigger of the collective AA framework has been reached per 72-hour NHC forecast at 7:00 p.m. Dec. 31, 2024 Cuba local time. Additional updates promised. Signed: OCHA Center for Humanitarian Data, The Hague.

--- page 35 ---
Annex 3: RC confirmation of Anticipatory Action window activation — Spanish + English. Main point: RC confirmation template for anticipatory action window, with conditional text depending on whether readiness trigger was previously reached.
Spanish: From RC; To CERF + agencies; Subject confirms ANTICIPATORY ACTION window activation. Body: INSMET/CHD analysis confirms action trigger reached; RC confirms activation; requests CERF next steps. Agencies will receive [if readiness NOT reached:] CERF approval letters with disbursement details; [if readiness WAS reached:] confirmation that they are authorized to use full CERF allocation.
English: Same content. Highlighted conditional clauses (yellow highlight in document): "in case the readiness trigger has not been reached" → CERF approval letters with disbursement details; "if the readiness trigger has been reached" → confirmation of authorization of resources by CERF. Signed: United Nations Resident Coordinator, Cuba.

--- page 36 ---
Annex 3: Observational / Early Response trigger CHD notification — Spanish + English. Main point: automated email template sent by OCHA CHD when observational trigger threshold reached (based on observed NHC track + NASA IMERG rainfall).
Spanish: From data.science@humdata.org; To: INSMET, OCHA, OCR; Subject: "Umbral del disparador OBSERVACIONAL alcanzado / OBSERVATIONAL trigger reached." Body: informs early response trigger reached per observational data (NHC observed track + IMERG precipitation) from last 48 hours; TEST_STORM_NAME, observación 19h00 31 dic 2024.
English version begins at bottom of page: "Cuba: Hurricane monitoring" — states observational/early response trigger reached per observational data from last 48 hours by NOAA NHC and NASA IMERG rainfall data.

--- page 37 ---
Annex 3: Observational trigger CHD notification — English version. Narrative text, no figure. Full English email from OCHA Center for Humanitarian Data: informs that the observational/early response trigger of the collective AA framework for hurricanes in Cuba has been reached, according to observational data issued during last 48 hours by NOAA's National Hurricane Center and NASA's IMERG rainfall data. Additional updates promised. Signed: OCHA Center for Humanitarian Data, The Hague. Contact: Raquel Peña, raquel.pena@un.org.

--- page 38 ---
Annex 3: RC confirmation of Early Response window activation — Spanish + English. Main point: RC confirmation template for early response window, with conditional text for whether prior readiness trigger was activated.
Spanish: From RC; To CERF + agencies; Subject confirms EARLY RESPONSE window activation. Body: based on scientific observations + INSMET/INRH/CHD analysis, early response trigger reached; RC confirms EARLY RESPONSE ("RESPUESTA TEMPRANA") window activation; requests CERF next steps; invites immediate implementation. Conditional: [if no prior thresholds reached] → CERF approval letters with disbursement details; [if readiness trigger was reached] → confirmation of authorization of resources by CERF.
English: Identical content — RC confirms activation of EARLY RESPONSE window; requests CERF proceed; agencies to immediately implement framework actions. Signed: United Nations Resident Coordinator, Cuba.
